event: The Mystical Marriage of Saint Catherine
symbols: ring placed on Catherine's finger — the mystical betrothal of the saint to Christ, total devotion
infant Jesus performing the marriage — Christ as divine bridegroom even from infancy
Franciscan saint kneeling — religious devotion and the monastic ideal of espousal to Christ
angels above — heavenly witnesses to the mystical union
red curtain — the royal and sacrificial canopy over the sacred event
description: A group of figures gathered in a landscape setting. At center, the Virgin Mary in a pink-red dress holds the infant Jesus, who reaches toward a young woman (Saint Catherine) kneeling at right in a white and gold gown, appearing to place or receive a ring. Joseph stands behind Mary at upper left in a grey-blue and red robe. A Franciscan friar kneels at lower left in a brown habit. Above, two cherubs or angels descend from golden-orange clouds. A red curtain drapes at the upper left. The background shows a lush green landscape with trees.
theological_description: The Mystical Marriage of Saint Catherine depicts the legendary vision in which the infant Christ, held by his mother, places a ring on the finger of Saint Catherine of Alexandria (or Siena), signifying her total spiritual consecration to him. While post-biblical, this devotional theme is deeply rooted in the scriptural metaphor of Christ as the Bridegroom of the Church and of the individual soul. Paul writes: 'I promised you to one husband, to Christ, so that I might present you as a pure virgin to him' (2 Corinthians 11:2). The Song of Solomon's bridal mysticism — 'My beloved is mine and I am his' (Song of Solomon 2:16) — provides the Old Testament foundation. Revelation 19:7-9 describes the eschatological marriage supper of the Lamb. Catherine's acceptance of the ring embodies the radical self-gift of consecrated life: leaving all to follow Christ (Matthew 19:29). The presence of Mary as intermediary reflects her role at Cana (John 2:1-5), where she facilitated the first sign at a wedding — itself a foreshadowing of the messianic banquet.

scripture_refs.reason: I promised you to one husband, to Christ, as a pure virgin — the theological basis of mystical marriage
My beloved is mine and I am his — the bridal mysticism underlying the scene
The marriage supper of the Lamb — the eschatological wedding that Catherine's espousal anticipates
Everyone who has left everything for my sake will receive a hundredfold — the consecrated life
